patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] accept an empty attribute argument list

version_history.subject: [PATCH] accept an empty attribute argument list

patches_submitted.patch_summary: The patch proposes to accept an empty attribute argument list in the Linux kernel, aligning with GCC and Clang behavior. This involves modifying the parsing of attributes to handle cases where no arguments are provided.

review_comments.author: Dan Carpenter
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the patch and added Tested-by tag, indicating it works for them but no technical review was provided.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Jun 05, 2026 at 11:30:45AM +0000, Dmitry Ilvokhin wrote:
> GCC and clang accept an empty argument list for attributes, e.g.
> __attribute__((nonnull())), and treat it the same as the bare
> __attribute__((nonnull)). sparse instead rejects it with
> "an expression is expected before ')'".
> 
> ignore_attribute() and recover_unknown_attribute() consume the optional
> argument list with parens_expression(), which requires a non-empty
> expression, so an empty () is rejected. The bare form is fine because it
> takes no parentheses and skips that path.
> 
> This turned up in the Linux kernel: the cleanup.h guard macros expand
> __nonnull_args() to __attribute__((__nonnull__())).
> 
>   https://lore.kernel.org/all/aiJi0WcYE8FZt-jO@stanley.mountain/
> 
> Reported-by: Dan Carpenter <error27@gmail.com>
> Signed-off-by: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>
> ---
>  parse.c                              | 17 +++++++++++++----
>  validation/attribute-nonnull-empty.c | 12 ++++++++++++
>  2 files changed, 25 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)
>  create mode 100644 validation/attribute-nonnull-empty.c

Thanks so much!

Tested-by: Dan Carpenter <error27@gmail.com>

regards,
dan carpenter

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 3/4] cleanup: Annotate guard constructors with nonnull

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 3/4] cleanup: Annotate guard constructors with nonnull

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new macro __nonnull_args() to annotate guard constructors in the Linux kernel, which warns the compiler when NULL is statically known to be passed as an argument. The annotation is applied to constructors that take pointer arguments, ensuring that the compiler checks for null pointers and preventing potential crashes or unexpected behavior.

review_comments.raw_body:
I am fine with either, but thanks for the caution! :)

I assume `_args()` is meant as "the `nonnull` for the arguments, not
the return"?

Cheers,

review_comments.author: tip-bot2 Ilvokhin
review_comments.summary: Reviewer tip-bot2 Ilvokhin noted that the __nonnull_args() macro is defined in compiler_attributes.h, but its usage in DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_1() does not include a parameter list (x...), which could lead to incorrect attribute application and potential compiler warnings.
review_comments.raw_body:
The following commit has been merged into the locking/core branch of tip:

Commit-ID:     22302af28d3f7c3ca38536978c80db51d2a9e283
Gitweb:        https://git.kernel.org/tip/22302af28d3f7c3ca38536978c80db51d2a9e283
Author:        Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>
AuthorDate:    Tue, 02 Jun 2026 07:12:52 
Committer:     Peter Zijlstra <peterz@infradead.org>
CommitterDate: Wed, 03 Jun 2026 11:38:48 +02:00

cleanup: Annotate guard constructors with nonnull

Add __nonnull_args() to unconditional guard constructors so the compiler
warns when NULL is statically known to be passed:

- DEFINE_GUARD(): re-declare the constructor with __nonnull_args().
- __DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_1(): annotate the constructor directly.

DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_0() needs no annotation: its constructor takes no
pointer arguments (.lock is hardcoded to (void *)1).

Define the __nonnull_args() macro in compiler_attributes.h, following
the existing convention for attribute wrappers. Deliberately not named
'__nonnull', to avoid clashing with glibc's __nonnull() when kernel and
userspace headers are combined (User Mode Linux for example).

Signed-off-by: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>
Signed-off-by: Peter Zijlstra (Intel) <peterz@infradead.org>
Link: https://patch.msgid.link/85fee12eec20abfcf711443518e8f0caec982a86.1780064327.git.d@ilvokhin.com
---
 include/linux/cleanup.h             |  4 +++-
 include/linux/compiler_attributes.h |  9 +++++++++
 2 files changed, 12 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)

diff --git a/include/linux/cleanup.h b/include/linux/cleanup.h
index ea95ca4..4e60d51 100644
--- a/include/linux/cleanup.h
+++ b/include/linux/cleanup.h
@@ -397,6 +397,7 @@ static __maybe_unused const bool class_##_name##_is_conditional = _is_cond
 	__DEFINE_GUARD_LOCK_PTR(_name, _T)
 
 #define DEFINE_GUARD(_name, _type, _lock, _unlock) \
+	static __always_inline __nonnull_args() _type class_##_name##_constructor(_type _T); \
 	DEFINE_CLASS(_name, _type, if (_T) { _unlock; }, ({ _lock; _T; }), _type _T); \
 	DEFINE_CLASS_IS_GUARD(_name)
 
@@ -497,7 +498,8 @@ static __always_inline void class_##_name##_destructor(class_##_name##_t *_T) \
 __DEFINE_GUARD_LOCK_PTR(_name, &_T->lock)
 
 #define __DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_1(_name, _type, ...)			\
-static __always_inline class_##_name##_t class_##_name##_constructor(_type *l) \
+static __always_inline __nonnull_args()					\
+class_##_name##_t class_##_name##_constructor(_type *l)			\
 	__no_context_analysis						\
 {									\
 	class_##_name##_t _t = { .lock = l }, *_T = &_t;		\
diff --git a/include/linux/compiler_attributes.h b/include/linux/compiler_attributes.h
index c16d419..cffe093 100644
--- a/include/linux/compiler_attributes.h
+++ b/include/linux/compiler_attributes.h
@@ -231,6 +231,15 @@
 #define   noinline                      __attribute__((__noinline__))
 
 /*
+ * Note: deliberately not named '__nonnull', to avoid clashing with glibc's
+ * __nonnull() when kernel and userspace headers are combined.
+ *
+ *   gcc: https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/gcc/Common-Attributes.html#index-nonnull
+ * clang: https://clang.llvm.org/docs/AttributeReference.html#nonnull
+ */
+#define __nonnull_args(x...)		__attribute__((__nonnull__(x)))
+
+/*
  * Optional: only supported since gcc >= 8
  * Optional: not supported by clang
  *

review_comments.author: Dan Carpenter
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Dan Carpenter noted that Sparse complains about an empty __nonnull_args() macro, suggesting it should be used with a specific argument like __nonnull_args(1) to specify which arguments are non-NULL.
review_comments.raw_body:
Sparse doesn't like an empty __nonnull_args() at all.

./include/linux/spinlock.h:608:1: error: an expression is expected before ')'
./include/linux/spinlock.h:619:1: error: an expression is expected before ')'
./include/linux/spinlock.h:631:1: error: an expression is expected before ')'

Shouldn't we specify the arguments which are non-NULL?
__nonnull_args(1)?

regards,
dan carpenter

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that sparse trips over the empty-parens form of the guard macro expansion, agreed to add explicit arguments for nonnull(), and provided a simple fix.

Author addressed Dan Carpenter's feedback that Sparse cannot parse the empty argument list in __nonnull_args(), agreed to specify the index explicitly by adding (1) to the macro call, and updated the patch accordingly.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for the report, Dan.

That is interesting, it seems sparse trips over the empty-parens form
the guard macro expands to, nonnull(), but is fine with
__attribute__((nonnull)) without explicit arguments, that a few places
in-tree already use. GCC and clang accept both, so this probably
deserves a fix in sparse too.

Meanwhile, spelling the argument out explicitly is a simple and
reasonable fix (and arguably clearer). Patch below.

---

From: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>
Date: Fri, 5 Jun 2026 03:06:22 -0700
Subject: [PATCH] cleanup: Specify nonnull argument index

The guard constructors were annotated with an empty __nonnull_args(),
relying on __nonnull__() marking every pointer parameter as non-NULL.
Sparse cannot parse the empty argument list.

Both constructors take the lock pointer as their first parameter, so
specify the index explicitly: __nonnull_args(1).

Reported-by: Dan Carpenter <error27@gmail.com>
Closes: https://lore.kernel.org/all/aiJi0WcYE8FZt-jO@stanley.mountain/
Signed-off-by: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>
---
 include/linux/cleanup.h | 4 ++--
 1 file changed, 2 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)

diff --git a/include/linux/cleanup.h b/include/linux/cleanup.h
index 65416938e318..b1b5698cbf1b 100644
--- a/include/linux/cleanup.h
+++ b/include/linux/cleanup.h
@@ -397,7 +397,7 @@ static __maybe_unused const bool class_##_name##_is_conditional = _is_cond
 	__DEFINE_GUARD_LOCK_PTR(_name, _T)
 
 #define DEFINE_GUARD(_name, _type, _lock, _unlock) \
-	static __always_inline __nonnull_args() _type class_##_name##_constructor(_type _T); \
+	static __always_inline __nonnull_args(1) _type class_##_name##_constructor(_type _T); \
 	DEFINE_CLASS(_name, _type, _unlock, ({ _lock; _T; }), _type _T); \
 	DEFINE_CLASS_IS_GUARD(_name)
 
@@ -498,7 +498,7 @@ static __always_inline void class_##_name##_destructor(class_##_name##_t *_T) \
 __DEFINE_GUARD_LOCK_PTR(_name, &_T->lock)
 
 #define __DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_1(_name, _type, ...)			\
-static __always_inline __nonnull_args()					\
+static __always_inline __nonnull_args(1)				\
 class_##_name##_t class_##_name##_constructor(_type *l)			\
 	__no_context_analysis						\
 {									\
-- 
2.53.0-Meta

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 0/9] dax/kmem: atomic whole-device hotplug via sysfs

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 0/9] dax/kmem: atomic whole-device hotplug via sysfs

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 1/9] mm/memory: add memory_block_aligned_range() helper
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new helper function called `memory_block_aligned_range()` to the Linux kernel's memory management code. The function takes an input range and aligns its start address up and end address down to memory block boundaries, which is required for memory hotplug operations. This helper function is added as a common interface in `<linux/memory.h>` and is used by the `dax/kmem` driver to ensure that memory ranges are properly aligned during hotplug operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 2/9] mm/memory_hotplug: pass online_type to online_memory_block() via arg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the `online_memory_block` function to accept an explicit online type through its argument, rather than relying on a default value. This change prepares for future scenarios where callers can specify custom online types. The patch also updates the `add_memory_resource` function to pass the default online type as a local variable. No functional changes are introduced by this patch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 3/9] mm/memory_hotplug: export mhp_get_default_online_type
series_items.patch_summary: This patch exports the `mhp_get_default_online_type` function from the Linux kernel's memory hotplug module, allowing drivers to use it even when hotplug is disabled. This is necessary for drivers that combine hotplug and DAX (Direct Access) use cases in the same code, as they need to be able to build and run without requiring hotplug to be enabled. The function simply returns `MMOP_OFFLINE` by default, which is a non-destructive operation.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 4/9] mm/memory_hotplug: add __add_memory_driver_managed() with online_type arg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new interface to the Linux kernel's memory hotplug functionality, allowing drivers to select their preferred online type (ZONE_NORMAL or ZONE_MOVABLE) when adding memory. The CXL driver is the primary motivator for this change, as it needs to plumb a preferred policy through the dax kmem driver. The patch refactors existing APIs to add a new helper function that accepts an explicit online type and validates its value. This change does not introduce any functional changes for existing users but provides a more flexible way for drivers to manage memory hotplug.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 5/9] mm/memory_hotplug: add multi-range hotunplug
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the ability to offline and remove multiple contiguous memory ranges as one operation, ensuring atomicity and preventing race conditions that could lead to inconsistent system states. The new function, offline_and_remove_memory_ranges(), takes an array of ranges and processes them under a single lock, allowing for all-or-nothing semantics in case of failures or interruptions. This addresses issues with current implementations that require calling offline_and_remove_memory() in a loop, which can give up atomicity and lead to system inconsistencies.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 6/9] dax: plumb hotplug online_type through dax
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the ability for drivers using dax_kmem to specify an auto-online policy when creating DAX devices. It does this by adding an 'online_type' field to the dev_dax_data structure, which can be set to a specific memory hotplug online type. This allows drivers to choose their preferred auto-online policy instead of relying on the system default. The patch makes no functional changes to existing callers and is intended to provide more flexibility for DAX device creation.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 7/9] dax/kmem: extract hotplug/hotremove helper functions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the kmem probe and remove functions in the dax/kmem driver by extracting initialization, cleanup, hotplug, and hot-remove logic into separate helper functions. The helpers enable future extensions to support more granular control over memory hotplug operations. The patch also addresses an issue where resources are not registered when probing occurs, preventing dax devices from being added to sysfs.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 8/9] dax/kmem: add sysfs interface for atomic hotplug
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a sysfs interface to control and query the state of an entire region of memory managed by the dax kmem driver. The new 'hotplug' attribute allows userspace to set the state of the memory region, which can be either online in one of three zones (ZONE_NORMAL, ZONE_MOVABLE, or automatically chosen), offline, or unplugged. The patch also introduces an atomic mechanism for offlining and removing all memory blocks together, eliminating a race condition that could cause hot-unplug to fail. Additionally, the patch updates the unbind routine to handle online memory more safely.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 9/9] selftests/dax: add dax/kmem hotplug sysfs regression test
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a selftest to verify the functionality of the dax/kmem hotplug sysfs attribute, which allows transitioning a kmem-backed dax device between 'unplugged', 'online' and 'online_movable' states. The test discovers an existing kmem-bound dax device, skips if none is found or memory cannot be freed, and then validates the interface contract by checking for correct behavior in various scenarios, including online -> unplug -> online_movable -> unplug cycle.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds atomic whole-device hotplug control via sysfs, allowing for transitions to be made atomically across entire device regions. The existing per-block toggling pattern is preserved for backwards compatibility but deprecated in favor of the new atomic approach. The patch introduces a 'hotplug' attribute and updates mm and dax plumbing to support it, including helpers for online_type and offline_and_remove_memory_ranges. This addresses a race condition between offlining and removing memory blocks, which was discussed at the LPC2025 device memory sessions.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/3] fuse: io-uring request completion and ordering fixes

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/3] fuse: io-uring request completion and ordering fixes

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/3] fuse: fix EFAULT clobber in fuse_uring_commit
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the fuse_uring_commit function where an EFAULT error code is clobbered by a positive residual value from copy_from_user(). The fix involves assigning err = -EFAULT before jumping to out, ensuring that fuse_uring_req_end() receives a negative errno and sets req->out.h.error to -EFAULT.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: Identified a common pitfall in using copy_from_user() and suggested setting up static checks to avoid similar issues in the future.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 6/5/26 21:27, Joanne Koong wrote:
> From: Chris Mason <clm@meta.com>
> 
> copy_from_user() returns the number of bytes not copied as an unsigned
> residual on failure (1..sizeof(struct fuse_out_header)). fuse_uring_commit
> stores that residual in ssize_t err, sets req->out.h.error to -EFAULT,
> then jumps to out: with err still holding the positive residual.
> 
>     err = copy_from_user(&req->out.h, &ent->headers->in_out,
>                          sizeof(req->out.h));
>     if (err) {
>         req->out.h.error = -EFAULT;
>         goto out;          /* err is the positive residual */
>     }
>     ...
>     out:
>         fuse_uring_req_end(ent, req, err);
> 
> fuse_uring_req_end() then runs
> 
>     if (error)
>         req->out.h.error = error;
> 
> which overwrites the just-assigned -EFAULT with the positive residual.
> FUSE callers such as fuse_simple_request() test err < 0 to detect
> failure, so the positive value is interpreted as success and the
> caller proceeds with an uninitialised or partial req->out.args.
> 
> Fix by assigning err = -EFAULT in the failure branch before jumping
> to out, so fuse_uring_req_end() receives a negative errno and sets
> req->out.h.error to -EFAULT.
> 
> Fixes: c090c8abae4b ("fuse: Add io-uring sqe commit and fetch support")
> Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
> Reviewed-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>
> Assisted-by: kres (claude-opus-4-7)
> Signed-off-by: Chris Mason <clm@meta.com>
> ---
>  fs/fuse/dev_uring.c | 9 +++------
>  1 file changed, 3 insertions(+), 6 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/fuse/dev_uring.c b/fs/fuse/dev_uring.c
> index e467b23e6895..e33847436693 100644
> --- a/fs/fuse/dev_uring.c
> +++ b/fs/fuse/dev_uring.c
> @@ -811,14 +811,11 @@ static void fuse_uring_commit(struct fuse_ring_ent *ent, struct fuse_req *req,
>  			      unsigned int issue_flags)
>  {
>  	struct fuse_ring *ring = ent->queue->ring;
> -	ssize_t err = 0;
> +	ssize_t err = -EFAULT;
>

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/3] fuse: fix data races on ring->ready
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses data races on ring->ready in the fuse kernel module, specifically on weakly-ordered architectures where stores can be reordered past loads. The fix involves upgrading the store to smp_store_release() and the load to smp_load_acquire(), ensuring that the preceding WRITE_ONCE(fiq->ops, ...) is visible to any CPU observing ring->ready == true.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: Provided a Reviewed-by tag without any specific comments or feedback.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 6/5/26 21:27, Joanne Koong wrote:
> From: Chris Mason <clm@meta.com>
> 
> On weakly-ordered architectures, the store to fiq->ops can be
> reordered past the store to ring->ready, allowing a CPU that sees
> ring->ready == true via fuse_uring_ready() to dispatch requests
> through a stale fiq->ops pointer. Upgrade the store to
> smp_store_release() and the load in fuse_uring_ready() to
> smp_load_acquire() so that the preceding WRITE_ONCE(fiq->ops, ...)
> is visible to any CPU that observes ring->ready == true.
> 
> Additionally, fuse_uring_do_register() publishes ring->ready with
> WRITE_ONCE() but the fast-path check reads it with a plain load.
> This is a marked-vs-unmarked access that KCSAN will flag. Wrap it in
> READ_ONCE() to mark it without adding unnecessary ordering.
> 
> Also wrap the fc->ring load in fuse_uring_ready() in READ_ONCE() to
> prevent the compiler from reloading it between the NULL check and the
> dereference.
> 
> Fixes: c2c9af9a0b13 ("fuse: Allow to queue fg requests through io-uring")
> Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
> Reviewed-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>
> Assisted-by: kres (claude-opus-4-7)
> Signed-off-by: Chris Mason <clm@meta.com>
> ---
>  fs/fuse/dev_uring.c   | 4 ++--
>  fs/fuse/dev_uring_i.h | 4 +++-
>  2 files changed, 5 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/fuse/dev_uring.c b/fs/fuse/dev_uring.c
> index e33847436693..7cd50990b097 100644
> --- a/fs/fuse/dev_uring.c
> +++ b/fs/fuse/dev_uring.c
> @@ -986,12 +986,12 @@ static void fuse_uring_do_register(struct fuse_ring_ent *ent,
>  	fuse_uring_ent_avail(ent, queue);
>  	spin_unlock(&queue->lock);
>  
> -	if (!ring->ready) {
> +	if (!READ_ONCE(ring->ready)) {
>  		bool ready = is_ring_ready(ring, queue->qid);
>  
>  		if (ready) {
>  			WRITE_ONCE(fiq->ops, &fuse_io_uring_ops);
> -			WRITE_ONCE(ring->ready, true);
> +			smp_store_release(&ring->ready, true);
>  			wake_up_all(&fch->blocked_waitq);
>  		}
>  	}
> diff --git a/fs/fuse/dev_uring_i.h b/fs/fuse/dev_uring_i.h
> index 368f4d0790eb..6af604e17b2d 100644
> --- a/fs/fuse/dev_uring_i.h
> +++ b/fs/fuse/dev_uring_i.h
> @@ -167,7 +167,9 @@ static inline void fuse_uring_wait_stopped_queues(struct fuse_chan *fch)
>  
>  static inline bool fuse_uring_ready(struct fuse_chan *fch)
>  {
> -	return fch->ring && fch->ring->ready;
> +	struct fuse_ring *ring = READ_ONCE(fch->ring);
> +
> +	return ring && smp_load_acquire(&ring->ready);
>  }
>  
>  #else /* CONFIG_FUSE_IO_URING */


Reviewed-by: Bernd Schubert <bernd@bsbernd.com>

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/3] fuse: end fuse_req on io-uring cancel task work
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the io-uring implementation for FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) where a request is not properly ended when the task work is cancelled, leading to incorrect behavior and potential stalls. The fix involves calling fuse_uring_req_end() before falling through to fuse_uring_send() in the cancel branch of fuse_uring_send_in_task(). This ensures that the request is correctly ended and its reference is discharged, preventing issues such as repeated cancels inflating a counter and stalling background operations.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 00/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 00/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 01/10] fuse: separate next request fetching from sending logic
series_items.patch_summary: This patch separates the logic of fetching the next request from sending it, making the control flow clearer and reducing unnecessary parameter passing. It removes duplicated code in fuse_uring_send_next_to_ring() and introduces a new function fuse_uring_get_next_fuse_req() to fetch the next request. The send operation is now handled by a separate function fuse_uring_send(). This change simplifies the logic for handling requests and improves maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 02/10] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying to ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers to a ring in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring implementation. It extracts the header copying into a new function called copy_header_to_ring(), which centralizes error handling and rate-limited logging, making the code more clear and maintainable. The patch modifies several functions that interact with the ring, replacing direct calls to copy_to_user() with calls to the new function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 03/10] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying from ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers from a ring in the fuse kernel module. It extracts this logic into a new function called copy_header_from_ring(), which makes it clearer and more centralized, and also handles errors and rate-limited logging. This change is part of a larger series of patches to improve the io-uring functionality in the fuse module.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 04/10] fuse: use enum types for header copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the interface for copying headers in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation by introducing enum types to identify which part of the header needs to be copied. This change will simplify both kernel-space and user-space header addresses copying when buffer rings are added, making it easier to manage and maintain the code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 05/10] fuse: refactor setting up copy state for payload copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the code for setting up copy state in the FUSE implementation of dev_uring by introducing a new helper function `setup_fuse_copy_state`. This function encapsulates the logic for importing user buffers and initializing the copy state. The existing code is simplified and made more modular, reducing duplication and improving maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 06/10] fuse: use named constants for io-uring iovec indices
series_items.patch_summary: This patch replaces magic indices 0 and 1 in the iovec array with named constants FUSE_URING_IOV_HEADERS and FUSE_URING_IOV_PAYLOAD, making the code self-documenting and preparing for future buffer ring support. The patch modifies several functions in the fuse module to use these new constants instead of hardcoded indices. This change improves code readability and maintainability by eliminating magic numbers and making it easier to understand the purpose of each index.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 07/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools (implemented as buffer ring)
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a buffer pool mechanism to the fuse io-uring implementation, allowing the server to pass in a contiguous allocation of buffers that can be used on demand by the kernel. This decouples the number of entries from the number of payload buffers required, reducing memory usage and enabling future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption. The server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec payload in the first sqe. The kernel manages buffer selection and recycling using a buffer ring data structure, which is partitioned into individual buffers according to the buf_size passed in at registration time.

review_comments.author: Baokun Li
review_comments.summary: Gave Reviewed-by

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 08/10] fuse: support registered buffers as buffer pools in io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for registered buffers as buffer pools in io-uring, allowing servers to use an io-uring registered buffer as the buffer pool and eliminating the overhead of pinning/unpinning user pages per i/o request. Registered buffers require enabling the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag and setting the FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFFERS init flag during registration. The patch also includes benchmark results showing a 10% throughput improvement for direct randreads, a 4% improvement for direct sequential reads, an 8% improvement for buffered randreads, and a 6% improvement for buffered sequential reads.

review_comments.author: Baokun Li
review_comments.summary: Gave Reviewed-by

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 09/10] fuse: add zero-copy over io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds zero-copy support to the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring implementation, allowing for direct access to client pages or page cache folios without intermediate memory copies. The kernel registers relevant pages into a sparse slot in the server's io_uring registered buffer table, enabling the server to operate on these pages directly using fixed buffer operations. This feature is gated behind CAP_SYS_ADMIN and FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFFERS, requiring bufpools and keeping configuration space small. Zero-copy allows the server direct access to client pages, eliminating the need for intermediary buffers or page cache folios.

review_comments.author: Baokun Li
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that io_buffer_unregister() is never called when a connection abort occurs during a zero-copy request, leaving the buf_table slot occupied and folios pinned until the io_uring ring is destroyed.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Joanne,

If a connection abort happens while a zero-copy request is in-flight,
io_buffer_unregister() is never called. The buf_table slot remains
occupied and folios stay pinned until the io_uring ring is destroyed.

So perhaps we need to add the corresponding cleanup in
fuse_uring_entry_teardown(). Otherwise looks good to me:

Reviewed-by: Baokun Li <libaokun@linux.alibaba.com>


Cheers,
Baokun

Thanks for reviewing this series.  That's a good point - the io buffer
is unregistered in fuse_uring_req_end() when a request is ended, but

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about unregistration of io-uring buffers during the abort race, explaining that it requires nontrivial changes to io-uring and would introduce complexity. They acknowledge that leaving the buffer table slot occupied until the io-uring ring is destroyed is acceptable.
review_comments.raw_body:
Revisiting this... I had this cleanup in the series I sent out last
December [1] but in order to accommodate unregistration on teardown
during the abort race, it requires nontrivial changes to io-uring, as
it requires operating on a ring ctx handle but it's tricky to
ascertain if the handle is valid or not. I had discussed it with Jens
at LPC last year, and we came to the conclusion that it's not worth
introducing the complexity to accommodate this specific scenario and
it's fine to leave the buffer table slot occupied with pinned folios
until the io-uring ring is destroyed.

Thanks,
Joanne

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 10/10] docs: fuse: add io-uring buffer pool and zero-copy documentation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation for using io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy techniques. The documentation explains how to use buffer pools, which decouple the relationship between entries and payload buffers, allowing for more optimal memory usage. It also describes how fuse manages buffer pools internally through a buffer ring, enabling features like incremental buffer consumption and multiple buffer sizes per ring. This patch aims to improve performance by reducing unnecessary copies of data between user space and kernel space.

prior to being passed to fuse at registration time). Imo the better
way if you wanted to dynamically add ents would be to add a new buffer
pool to the ring.

Please take a look at patch 8 ([PATCH v3 08/10] fuse: support
registered buffers as buffer pools in io-uring) [1]. The buffer pool
is contiguously allocated and registered as an io-uring registered
buffer, and then passed to fuse and by that point, the buffers have
already been pinned by io-uring.

buffers contiguously up front as a pool instead of per-ent, as the
purpose of this series is to decouple the 1:1 entry:buffer mapping. Eg
if the request an ent is servicing does not require buffers, a buffer
should not be reserved for that ent, amongst other benefits of a
decoupled mapping.

I think perhaps there is a misunderstanding of the patches in this series.

This series implements buffer pools, which decouple the 1:1 mapping
between ents and buffers that currently exists. Right now, ents and
buffers are tightly coupled where each entry has its own dedicated
payload buffer, requiring N buffers for N entries where each buffer
must be large enough to accomodate the maximum payload size.

This series allows servers to pass in a buffer pool where the buffers
are not statically mapped to an ent at registration time. This
decoupling reduces the memory usage requirements needed to use
fuse-io-uring. It is the necessary stepping stone for future
optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption (allowing

On Fri, May 22, 2026 at 1:59 PM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> This series adds buffer pools (implemented internally as a buffer ring) and
> zero-copy to fuse io-uring.
>
> Prior to this series, ents and buffers are tightly coupled where each entry
> has its own dedicated payload buffer, requiring N buffers for N entries where
> each buffer must be large enough to accomodate the maximum payload size. This
> is suboptimal as most request types require vastly less bytes than the maximum
> payload size and some requests do not require payload buffers at all.
>
> This series allows servers to pass in a buffer pool (internally managed as a
> buffer ring) that the kernel will use for servicing ents/requests. This
> decoupling reduces the memory usage requirements needed to use fuse-io-uring.
> It allows for future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption and
> supporting multiple buffer sizes per ring.
>
> This series also allows servers to use io-uring registered buffers as the
> buffer pool which eliminates the overhead of pinning/unpinning user pages and
> translating virtual addreses per i/o request.
>
> This series additionally adds zero copy to fuse io-uring. The server can
> directly access client pages or page cache folios without copying data through
> an intermediary buffer. This requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges and using
> registered buffers.
>
> This series has a dependency on io-uring registered bvec buffers changes
> in [1].
>
> The throughput improvements from registered buffers and zero-copy depends on
> how much of the server's per-request latency is spent on data copying vs
> backing I/O. When backing I/O dominates, the saved memcpy is a negligible
> fraction of overall latency. Please also note that for the server to
> read/write into the zero-copied pages, the read/write must go through io-uring
> as an IORING_OP_READ_FIXED / IORING_OP_WRITE_FIXED operation. If the
> server's backing I/O is instantaneous (eg served from cache), the
> overhead of the additional io_uring operation may negate the savings
> from eliminating the memcpy.
>
> In benchmarks using passthrough_hp on a high-performance NVMe-backed
> system, using registered buffers showed around a 10% throughput improvement
> for direct randreads (~2150 MiB/s to ~2400 MiB/s), a 4% improvement for direct
> sequential reads (~2510 MiB/s to ~2620 MiB/s), a 8% improvement for buffered
> randreads (~2100 MiB/s to ~2280 MiB/s), and a 6% improvement for buffered
> sequential reads (~2500 MiB/s to ~2670 MiB/s).
>
> Zero-copy showed around a 35% throughput improvement for direct
> randreads (~2150 MiB/s to ~2900 MiB/s), a 15% improvement for direct
> sequential reads (~2510 MiB/s to ~2900 MiB/s), a 15% improvement for
> buffered randreads (~2100 MiB/s to ~2470 MiB/s), and a 10% improvement
> for buffered sequential reads (~2500 MiB/s to ~2750 MiB/s). I didn't see
> enough of a clear improvement for writes due to write latency being I/O
> dominated.
>
> The benchmarks were run using:
>   fio --name=test_run --ioengine=sync --rw=rand{read,write} --bs=1M
>   --size=1G --numjobs=2 --ramp_time=30 --group_reporting=1
>
> The libfuse changes can be found in [2]. To test the server, run:
> sudo ~/libfuse/build/example/passthrough_hp ~/src ~/mounts/tmp
> --nopassthrough -o io_uring_zero_copy -o io_uring_q_depth=8
> Once this series is merged, the libfuse changes will be tidied up and
> submitted upstream.
>
> Thanks,
> Joanne
>
> [1] https://lore.kernel.org/io-uring/20260402160929.2749744-1-joannelkoong@gmail.com/T/#t
> [2] https://github.com/joannekoong/libfuse/commits/zero_copy_v3/
>

Miklos,

This series has stalled on an architectural question and I'd
appreciate your input on the direction.

Background:
In fuse io-uring, entries are reusable communication slots between the
kernel and the daemon. When a request needs to be sent from the kernel
to the daemon, it is assigned to an available entry. The entry carries
the request to userspace, and the response comes back through the same
entry. The number of entries determines how many requests can be
serviced concurrently. Currently, when the daemon registers an entry
during fuse io-uring setup, it passes in a payload buffer (sized to
the maximum payload, typically 1 MiB) alongside it. The buffer is tied
to the entry for the lifetime of the connection.

Problem that needs to be solved:
Most request types (lookup, getattr, open, etc) require far less than
the maximum payload size, and some (flush, fsync, etc) require no
payload buffer at all. Every entry reserves a full 1 MiB buffer
regardless of actual need. On systems with many entries, this memory
cost scales linearly - for example, 64 entries across 8 queues
requires 512 MiB of payload buffers alone.

Approach A:
Decouple entries from payload buffers. Entries become generic - any
entry can use any buffer from the pool. At setup time, the daemon
passes in a contiguous pool of memory, and the kernel dynamically
selects and recycles buffers from this pool as entries are assigned
requests. Entries servicing requests with no payloads consume no
buffer, and the pool can have fewer buffers than entries. This
supports adding multi-size buffers and incremental buffer consumption
(multiple entries using non-overlapping regions of the same buffer
concurrently) as optimizations in the future.

Approach B:
Keep the 1:1 entry-buffer coupling, but allow entries to be registered
with different payload buffer sizes at setup time. The daemon would
register entries with smaller buffers for metadata-heavy workloads and
larger buffers for data-heavy ones. This keeps the existing model of
passing in a payload buffer during entry registration at setup time
and requires no uapi additions. Each entry registration adds buffer
capacity whereas with buffer pools, buffer capacity would have to be
added as a separate pool.

I want to make sure I'm representing both approaches fairly - Bernd,
please correct anything I've gotten wrong.

Miklos, what are your thoughts?

Thanks,
Joanne

The author confirmed that this is gated behind CAP_SYS_ADMIN because it allows the server direct access to the client's underlying pages.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the architectural direction of the fuse io-uring implementation, specifically regarding the reusability of communication slots between the kernel and daemon. The author appreciates Miklos' input on this matter and would like to discuss further.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Jun 1, 2026 at 3:37 PM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> On Mon, Jun 1, 2026 at 1:53 PM Bernd Schubert <bernd@bsbernd.com> wrote:
> >
> > On 6/1/26 22:19, Joanne Koong wrote:
> > > On Fri, May 29, 2026 at 2:27 PM Bernd Schubert <bernd@bsbernd.com> wrote:
> > >>
> > >>
> > >>
> > >> On 5/29/26 18:32, Joanne Koong wrote:
> > >>> On Fri, May 22, 2026 at 1:59 PM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
> > >>>>
> > >>>> This series adds buffer pools (implemented internally as a buffer ring) and
> > >>>> zero-copy to fuse io-uring.
> > >>>>
> > >>>> Prior to this series, ents and buffers are tightly coupled where each entry
> > >>>> has its own dedicated payload buffer, requiring N buffers for N entries where
> > >>>> each buffer must be large enough to accomodate the maximum payload size. This
> > >>>> is suboptimal as most request types require vastly less bytes than the maximum
> > >>>> payload size and some requests do not require payload buffers at all.
> > >>>>
> > >>>> This series allows servers to pass in a buffer pool (internally managed as a
> > >>>> buffer ring) that the kernel will use for servicing ents/requests. This
> > >>>> decoupling reduces the memory usage requirements needed to use fuse-io-uring.
> > >>>> It allows for future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption and
> > >>>> supporting multiple buffer sizes per ring.
> > >>>>
> > >>>> This series also allows servers to use io-uring registered buffers as the
> > >>>> buffer pool which eliminates the overhead of pinning/unpinning user pages and
> > >>>> translating virtual addreses per i/o request.
> > >>>>
> > >>>> This series additionally adds zero copy to fuse io-uring. The server can
> > >>>> directly access client pages or page cache folios without copying data through
> > >>>> an intermediary buffer. This requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges and using
> > >>>> registered buffers.
> > >>>>
> > >>>> This series has a dependency on io-uring registered bvec buffers changes
> > >>>> in [1].
> > >>>>
> > >>>> The throughput improvements from registered buffers and zero-copy depends on
> > >>>> how much of the server's per-request latency is spent on data copying vs
> > >>>> backing I/O. When backing I/O dominates, the saved memcpy is a negligible
> > >>>> fraction of overall latency. Please also note that for the server to
> > >>>> read/write into the zero-copied pages, the read/write must go through io-uring
> > >>>> as an IORING_OP_READ_FIXED / IORING_OP_WRITE_FIXED operation. If the
> > >>>> server's backing I/O is instantaneous (eg served from cache), the
> > >>>> overhead of the additional io_uring operation may negate the savings
> > >>>> from eliminating the memcpy.
> > >>>>
> > >>>> In benchmarks using passthrough_hp on a high-performance NVMe-backed
> > >>>> system, using registered buffers showed around a 10% throughput improvement
> > >>>> for direct randreads (~2150 MiB/s to ~2400 MiB/s), a 4% improvement for direct
> > >>>> sequential reads (~2510 MiB/s to ~2620 MiB/s), a 8% improvement for buffered
> > >>>> randreads (~2100 MiB/s to ~2280 MiB/s), and a 6% improvement for buffered
> > >>>> sequential reads (~2500 MiB/s to ~2670 MiB/s).
> > >>>>
> > >>>> Zero-copy showed around a 35% throughput improvement for direct
> > >>>> randreads (~2150 MiB/s to ~2900 MiB/s), a 15% improvement for direct
> > >>>> sequential reads (~2510 MiB/s to ~2900 MiB/s), a 15% improvement for
> > >>>> buffered randreads (~2100 MiB/s to ~2470 MiB/s), and a 10% improvement
> > >>>> for buffered sequential reads (~2500 MiB/s to ~2750 MiB/s). I didn't see
> > >>>> enough of a clear improvement for writes due to write latency being I/O
> > >>>> dominated.
> > >>>>
> > >>>> The benchmarks were run using:
> > >>>>   fio --name=test_run --ioengine=sync --rw=rand{read,write} --bs=1M
> > >>>>   --size=1G --numjobs=2 --ramp_time=30 --group_reporting=1
> > >>>>
> > >>>> The libfuse changes can be found in [2]. To test the server, run:
> > >>>> sudo ~/libfuse/build/example/passthrough_hp ~/src ~/mounts/tmp
> > >>>> --nopassthrough -o io_uring_zero_copy -o io_uring_q_depth=8
> > >>>> Once this series is merged, the libfuse changes will be tidied up and
> > >>>> submitted upstream.
> > >>>>
> > >>>> Thanks,
> > >>>> Joanne
> > >>>>
> > >>>> [1] https://lore.kernel.org/io-uring/20260402160929.2749744-1-joannelkoong@gmail.com/T/#t
> > >>>> [2] https://github.com/joannekoong/libfuse/commits/zero_copy_v3/
> > >>>>
> > >>>
> > >>> Miklos,
> > >>>
> > >>> This series has stalled on an architectural question and I'd
> > >>> appreciate your input on the direction.
> > >>>
> > >>> Background:
> > >>> In fuse io-uring, entries are reusable communication slots between the
> > >>> kernel and the daemon. When a request needs to be sent from the kernel
> > >>> to the daemon, it is assigned to an available entry. The entry carries
> > >>> the request to userspace, and the response comes back through the same
> > >>> entry. The number of entries determines how many requests can be
> > >>> serviced concurrently. Currently, when the daemon registers an entry
> > >>> during fuse io-uring setup, it passes in a payload buffer (sized to
> > >>> the maximum payload, typically 1 MiB) alongside it. The buffer is tied
> > >>> to the entry for the lifetime of the connection.
> > >>>
> > >>> Problem that needs to be solved:
> > >>> Most request types (lookup, getattr, open, etc) require far less than
> > >>> the maximum payload size, and some (flush, fsync, etc) require no
> > >>> payload buffer at all. Every entry reserves a full 1 MiB buffer
> > >>> regardless of actual need. On systems with many entries, this memory
> > >>> cost scales linearly - for example, 64 entries across 8 queues
> > >>> requires 512 MiB of payload buffers alone.
> > >>>
> > >>> Approach A:
> > >>> Decouple entries from payload buffers. Entries become generic - any
> > >>> entry can use any buffer from the pool. At setup time, the daemon
> > >>> passes in a contiguous pool of memory, and the kernel dynamically
> > >>> selects and recycles buffers from this pool as entries are assigned
> > >>> requests. Entries servicing requests with no payloads consume no
> > >>> buffer, and the pool can have fewer buffers than entries. This
> > >>> supports adding multi-size buffers and incremental buffer consumption
> > >>> (multiple entries using non-overlapping regions of the same buffer
> > >>> concurrently) as optimizations in the future.
> > >>>
> > >>> Approach B:
> > >>> Keep the 1:1 entry-buffer coupling, but allow entries to be registered
> > >>> with different payload buffer sizes at setup time. The daemon would
> > >>> register entries with smaller buffers for metadata-heavy workloads and
> > >>> larger buffers for data-heavy ones. This keeps the existing model of
> > >>> passing in a payload buffer during entry registration at setup time
> > >>> and requires no uapi additions. Each entry registration adds buffer
> > >>> capacity whereas with buffer pools, buffer capacity would have to be
> > >>> added as a separate pool.
> > >>>
> > >>> I want to make sure I'm representing both approaches fairly - Bernd,
> > >>> please correct anything I've gotten wrong.
> > >>
> > >> I think for B "at setup time" is not entirely correct - buffers in the
> > >> current model buffers and entries can be registered at run time.
> > >>
> > >>
> > >> I have been thinking about approach C, which I think is in between A and B.
> > >> I my mind approach C does the registration per entry, but payload
> > >> buffers get added to a buf array, if a payload is set.
> > >> Max array size would be negotiated as in your approach or maybe better
> > >> with a config SQE. Different IO size could achieved by multiple buf arrays.
> > >
> > > Ok, so it looks like we agree that entries should be decoupled from
> > > buffers. Now it looks like the remaining disagreement is how the
> > > buffers get passed in.
> >
> > Well let's say, I don't see what speaks against decoupling it. The
> > memory saving effect is due to the special form of different request
> > sizes (0 and max payload).
> >
> > >
> > > I still think it's simpler and cleaner to pass in the buffers as a
> > > contiguous pool upfront instead of passing in each buffer
> > > individually.
> >
> > My issue with this approach is still the same, incrementally registering
> > memory when one needs it is hard. Example, I have a few external SSDs
> > that are connected most of the time, but I only need them every few
> > days. Why would I need to spend memory for the fuse transport all the
> > time? The ideal situation would be: libfuse detects heavy usage and
> > registers more entries and buffers.
> > Current fuse kernel supports it. I just didn't get to it to implement
> > dynamic registration in libfuse yet - a matter of time.
> > Missing is some form of kernel unregister, although easy to add, if
> > unregister is treated as request OP.
> >
> > With a contiguous buffer pool I wouldn't know how to register on demand
> > and unregistering is even harder.
>
> For the scenario you mentioned above, you could register/unregister a
> buffer pool within one queue or alternatively, register/unregister a
> new queue, depending on the workload. I think the details can be
> worked out once we agree on the core design, but I don't think it
> would be more complicated with contiguous buffer pools.
>
> >
> > Also, if we still have races during register phase, doing it on demand
> > wouldn't change anything in the current scheme - either the race is
> > there or it is not, because current scheme is dynamic registration from
> > kernel point of view.
> >
> >
> > No we need to keep that old scheme one way or the other, but we could
> > deprecate one form and avoid adding new features (like different buffer
> > sizes). However, if buffer pools have a limited usability, once needs to
> > do those features for both approaches.
> >
> > >
> > > Miklos, what are your thoughts?
> >
> > Just a short reminder that it was actually Miklos to request the dynamic
> > registration way. Initially it was all static, taken from the ublk tag
> > model. Making it dynamic added some headache, but in the end I also
> > noticed that it can be useful.
>
> I wasn't there when that conversation happened a few years ago, so I'm
> not sure if Miklos's stance still pertains here where ents are
> decoupled from buffers. I think we need Miklos to clarify this for us.
>
> Thanks,
> Joanne
>

Miklos,

With entries decoupled from buffers, the remaining question is how
buffers should be passed in:

Contiguously: daemon passes in a contiguous pool upfront
Per-buffer: daemon passes buffers in individually

In my opinion, passing in the buffers contiguously as a pool leads to
simpler and cleaner logic at the fuse, io-uring, and userspace layers.
If a daemon wants to dynamically add/remove buffer capacity in the
future, they can add/remove another buffer pool or another ring,
depending on their workload.

>From Bernd's perspective, passing in each buffer individually allows
daemons who want to dynamically add/remove buffer capacity in the
future to add/remove capacity one buffer at a time based on load
instead of needing to commit upfront to adding/removing a pool.

Could you please share your preference? This series is targeting the
quickly approaching 7.2 merge window.

Thanks,
Joanne

> >
> >
> > Thanks,
> > Bernd
> >
> >

>> Regarding page mapping/pinning, why does it matter if the buffer in
>> kernel is known as per entry or per allocation (assuming payload buffers
>> are page aligned)?
> 
> It does matter how the buffers are passed to fuse for page pinning.
> Please take a look at patch 8 ([PATCH v3 08/10] fuse: support
> registered buffers as buffer pools in io-uring) [1]. The buffer pool
> is contiguously allocated and registered as an io-uring registered
> buffer, and then passed to fuse and by that point, the buffers have
> already been pinned by io-uring.
> 
> Even irrespective of page pinning, it would still be good to pass the
> buffers contiguously up front as a pool instead of per-ent, as the
> purpose of this series is to decouple the 1:1 entry:buffer mapping. Eg
> if the request an ent is servicing does not require buffers, a buffer
> should not be reserved for that ent, amongst other benefits of a
> decoupled mapping.
> 
> [1] https://lore.kernel.org/fuse-devel/20260522205823.1597313-9-joannelkoong@gmail.com/
> 
>>
>> I think the more important part for buf rings are pinned headers. For 4K
>> pages the loss is maybe still acceptable and the remaining size could be
>> used for small requests and I guess would have a perf benefit. For 64K
>> pages the loss is quite a lot.
> 
> That is orthogonal to this series. This series focuses on payload
> buffers, and header pinning is not part of this series as
> bufpools/bufrings do not need pinned headers, as it makes no
> difference for performance. As mentioned in the previous message, when
> I was working on a separate series for removing the dispatch overhead,
> I implemented header pinning as part of that, as that is when header
> pinning becomes actually useful.

Yes, header pinning is orthogonal, with the exception that ring entries
of different payload sizes provide the same feature as buffer pools and
with that buffer pools would not be a requirement anymore.

> 
>>
>>
>> Maybe let's summarize
>>
>> Bufrings
>> - Fixed size, allocated at startup, cannot be easily increased or decreased
>> - Allows 0 size payloads (FUSE requests that only need the header)
>>
>> Entries with payload
>> - Allows more flexible setup - fuse server _could_ (not implemented as
>> of now) create entries based on incoming IO.
>>
>> Neither provides real multi-size buffers per ring.
>>
> 
> I think perhaps there is a misunderstanding of the patches in this series.
> 
> This series implements buffer pools, which decouple the 1:1 mapping
> between ents and buffers that currently exists. Right now, ents and
> buffers are tightly coupled where each entry has its own dedicated
> payload buffer, requiring N buffers for N entries where each buffer
> must be large enough to accomodate the maximum payload size.
> 
> This series allows servers to pass in a buffer pool where the buffers
> are not statically mapped to an ent at registration time. This
> decoupling reduces the memory usage requirements needed to use
> fuse-io-uring. It is the necessary stepping stone for future
> optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption (allowing
> multiple entries to use non-overlapping regions of the same buffer
> concurrently) and supporting multi-size buffers per ring.

I just don't see "reduces the memory usage" - there are few op code that
do not need payload buffers. Do you see that exactly these op code
disturb your workflow and you do need additional entries/buffers for
those? I do see a lot of lookup/revalidate/getxattr, but not the op
codes without payload. Equally I see IO that does not max payload for a
lot of requests types - anything namespace related and reads/writes
below max payload size.

In the end, what I'm struggling with is that the "zero payload" is just
one special case of different IO sizes and the entire code and flow is
changed for this one special case, without a discussion forehand how to
handle the other cases. And that one special case comes up rarely in my
own testing and use cases.


Thanks,
Bernd

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: Bernd Schubert noted that the current implementation ties each entry in fuse io-uring to a full 1 MiB payload buffer, regardless of actual need, resulting in excessive memory usage on systems with many entries. He proposed decoupling entries from payload buffers as an approach to solve this problem.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 5/29/26 18:32, Joanne Koong wrote:
> On Fri, May 22, 2026 at 1:59\u202fPM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>>
>> This series adds buffer pools (implemented internally as a buffer ring) and
>> zero-copy to fuse io-uring.
>>
>> Prior to this series, ents and buffers are tightly coupled where each entry
>> has its own dedicated payload buffer, requiring N buffers for N entries where
>> each buffer must be large enough to accomodate the maximum payload size. This
>> is suboptimal as most request types require vastly less bytes than the maximum
>> payload size and some requests do not require payload buffers at all.
>>
>> This series allows servers to pass in a buffer pool (internally managed as a
>> buffer ring) that the kernel will use for servicing ents/requests. This
>> decoupling reduces the memory usage requirements needed to use fuse-io-uring.
>> It allows for future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption and
>> supporting multiple buffer sizes per ring.
>>
>> This series also allows servers to use io-uring registered buffers as the
>> buffer pool which eliminates the overhead of pinning/unpinning user pages and
>> translating virtual addreses per i/o request.
>>
>> This series additionally adds zero copy to fuse io-uring. The server can
>> directly access client pages or page cache folios without copying data through
>> an intermediary buffer. This requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges and using
>> registered buffers.
>>
>> This series has a dependency on io-uring registered bvec buffers changes
>> in [1].
>>
>> The throughput improvements from registered buffers and zero-copy depends on
>> how much of the server's per-request latency is spent on data copying vs
>> backing I/O. When backing I/O dominates, the saved memcpy is a negligible
>> fraction of overall latency. Please also note that for the server to
>> read/write into the zero-copied pages, the read/write must go through io-uring
>> as an IORING_OP_READ_FIXED / IORING_OP_WRITE_FIXED operation. If the
>> server's backing I/O is instantaneous (eg served from cache), the
>> overhead of the additional io_uring operation may negate the savings
>> from eliminating the memcpy.
>>
>> In benchmarks using passthrough_hp on a high-performance NVMe-backed
>> system, using registered buffers showed around a 10% throughput improvement
>> for direct randreads (~2150 MiB/s to ~2400 MiB/s), a 4% improvement for direct
>> sequential reads (~2510 MiB/s to ~2620 MiB/s), a 8% improvement for buffered
>> randreads (~2100 MiB/s to ~2280 MiB/s), and a 6% improvement for buffered
>> sequential reads (~2500 MiB/s to ~2670 MiB/s).
>>
>> Zero-copy showed around a 35% throughput improvement for direct
>> randreads (~2150 MiB/s to ~2900 MiB/s), a 15% improvement for direct
>> sequential reads (~2510 MiB/s to ~2900 MiB/s), a 15% improvement for
>> buffered randreads (~2100 MiB/s to ~2470 MiB/s), and a 10% improvement
>> for buffered sequential reads (~2500 MiB/s to ~2750 MiB/s). I didn't see
>> enough of a clear improvement for writes due to write latency being I/O
>> dominated.
>>
>> The benchmarks were run using:
>>   fio --name=test_run --ioengine=sync --rw=rand{read,write} --bs=1M
>>   --size=1G --numjobs=2 --ramp_time=30 --group_reporting=1
>>
>> The libfuse changes can be found in [2]. To test the server, run:
>> sudo ~/libfuse/build/example/passthrough_hp ~/src ~/mounts/tmp
>> --nopassthrough -o io_uring_zero_copy -o io_uring_q_depth=8
>> Once this series is merged, the libfuse changes will be tidied up and
>> submitted upstream.
>>
>> Thanks,
>> Joanne
>>
>> [1] https://lore.kernel.org/io-uring/20260402160929.2749744-1-joannelkoong@gmail.com/T/#t
>> [2] https://github.com/joannekoong/libfuse/commits/zero_copy_v3/
>>
> 
> Miklos,
> 
> This series has stalled on an architectural question and I'd
> appreciate your input on the direction.
> 
> Background:
> In fuse io-uring, entries are reusable communication slots between the
> kernel and the daemon. When a request needs to be sent from the kernel
> to the daemon, it is assigned to an available entry. The entry carries
> the request to userspace, and the response comes back through the same
> entry. The number of entries determines how many requests can be
> serviced concurrently. Currently, when the daemon registers an entry
> during fuse io-uring setup, it passes in a payload buffer (sized to
> the maximum payload, typically 1 MiB) alongside it. The buffer is tied
> to the entry for the lifetime of the connection.
> 
> Problem that needs to be solved:
> Most request types (lookup, getattr, open, etc) require far less than
> the maximum payload size, and some (flush, fsync, etc) require no
> payload buffer at all. Every entry reserves a full 1 MiB buffer
> regardless of actual need. On systems with many entries, this memory
> cost scales linearly - for example, 64 entries across 8 queues
> requires 512 MiB of payload buffers alone.
> 
> Approach A:
> Decouple entries from payload buffers. Entries become generic - any
> entry can use any buffer from the pool. At setup time, the daemon
> passes in a contiguous pool of memory, and the kernel dynamically
> selects and recycles buffers from this pool as entries are assigned
> requests. Entries servicing requests with no payloads consume no
> buffer, and the pool can have fewer buffers than entries. This
> supports adding multi-size buffers and incremental buffer consumption
> (multiple entries using non-overlapping regions of the same buffer
> concurrently) as optimizations in the future.
> 
> Approach B:
> Keep the 1:1 entry-buffer coupling, but allow entries to be registered
> with different payload buffer sizes at setup time. The daemon would
> register entries with smaller buffers for metadata-heavy workloads and
> larger buffers for data-heavy ones. This keeps the existing model of
> passing in a payload buffer during entry registration at setup time
> and requires no uapi additions. Each entry registration adds buffer
> capacity whereas with buffer pools, buffer capacity would have to be
> added as a separate pool.
> 
> I want to make sure I'm representing both approaches fairly - Bernd,
> please correct anything I've gotten wrong.

I think for B "at setup time" is not entirely correct - buffers in the
current model buffers and entries can be registered at run time.


I have been thinking about approach C, which I think is in between A and B.
I my mind approach C does the registration per entry, but payload
buffers get added to a buf array, if a payload is set.
Max array size would be negotiated as in your approach or maybe better
with a config SQE. Different IO size could achieved by multiple buf arrays.


Thanks,
Bernd

review_comments.author: Baokun Li
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Baokun Li pointed out a minor typo in the documentation, specifically that 'the iovec payload in in the first sqe' has a duplicated 'in'.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 2026/5/23 04:58, Joanne Koong wrote:
> Add documentation for fuse over io-uring usage of buffer pools and
> zero-copy.
>
> Reviewed-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>

Looks good to me, just one nit below:

Reviewed-by: Baokun Li <libaokun@linux.alibaba.com>

> ---
>  .../filesystems/fuse/fuse-io-uring.rst        | 194 ++++++++++++++++++
>  1 file changed, 194 insertions(+)
>
> diff --git a/Documentation/filesystems/fuse/fuse-io-uring.rst b/Documentation/filesystems/fuse/fuse-io-uring.rst
> index d73dd0dbd238..79003ddd44fe 100644
> --- a/Documentation/filesystems/fuse/fuse-io-uring.rst
> +++ b/Documentation/filesystems/fuse/fuse-io-uring.rst
> @@ -95,5 +95,199 @@ Sending requests with CQEs
>   |    <fuse_unlink()                         |
>   |  <sys_unlink()                            |
>  
> +Buffer pools
> +============
>  
> +Buffer pools decouple the 1:1 relationship between ents and payload buffers,
> +which allow for more optimal usage of memory. Entries only 'reserve' a buffer
> +when actively processing a request that requires a payload buffer. This
> +decoupling also allows for optimizations such as incremental buffer
> +consumption which will allow non-overlapping regions of a single buffer to be
> +used simultaneously across multiple ents/requests, and the possibility of
> +multiple buffer sizes to be supported per ring in the future.
>  
> +To use this, the server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during
> +registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec payload in
> +in the first sqe. The server needs to pass init.buf_size and init.nr_bufs

Nit: "the iovec payload in in the first sqe" has a duplicated "in".


> +describing the buffer pool configuration in the struct fuse_uring_cmd_req it
> +passes to the kernel. Buffer pool usage is set on a per-ring basis. Please
> +note that all subsequent registration SQEs for the same ring must pass in
> +consistent flags.
> +
> +Internally, fuse manages the buffer pool through a buffer ring. At a
> +high-level, this is how it works:
> +
> +* The first REGISTER SQE for a queue creates the queue and sets up the
> +  buffer pool. The server passes in the allocated buffer pool in the first
> +  registration sqe in iovec[1].
> +* When a client request arrives, if the request has copyable data, the kernel
> +  selects a buffer from the ring, copies headers and payload data, and
> +  completes the sqe.
> +* The buf_id of the selected payload buffer is communicated to the server
> +  via the fuse_uring_ent_in_out header. The server uses this to locate the
> +  payload buffer corresponding to the request.
> +* The server processes the request and sends a COMMIT_AND_FETCH SQE with
> +  the reply. The kernel processes the reply and recycles the buffer.
> +
> +Visually, this looks like::
> +
> + Payload buffer pool:
> + +-----------+-----------+-----------+-----+
> + | buf 0     | buf 1     | buf 2     | ... |
> + | (buf_size)| (buf_size)| (buf_size)|     |
> + +-----------+-----------+-----------+-----+
> + selected on demand, recycled after each request
> +
> +Buffer pool request flow
> +------------------------::
> +
> +|  Kernel                                  |  FUSE daemon
> +|                                          |
> +|  [client request arrives]                |
> +|  >fuse_uring_send()                      |
> +|    [select payload buf from pool]        |
> +|    >fuse_uring_select_buffer()           |
> +|    >copy_header_to_ring()                |
> +|    [copy payload to selected buf]        |
> +|    >fuse_uring_copy_to_ring()            |
> +|    [set buf_id in ent_in_out header]     |
> +|    >io_uring_cmd_done()                  |
> +|                                          |  [CQE received]
> +|                                          |  [read header]
> +|                                          |  [read payload from buf_id]
> +|                                          |  [process request]
> +|                                          |  [write reply to header]
> +|                                          |  [write reply payload to buf]
> +|                                          |  >io_uring_submit()
> +|                                          |   COMMIT_AND_FETCH
> +|  >fuse_uring_commit_fetch()              |
> +|    >fuse_uring_commit()                  |
> +|     [copy reply from ring]               |
> +|     >fuse_uring_recycle_buffer()         |
> +|    >fuse_uring_get_next_fuse_req()       |
> +
> +Registered buffers
> +==================
> +
> +Servers can pass in an io-uring registered buffer as the buffer pool. When the
> +server registers buffers with io-uring, the pages backing the buffers are
> +pinned upfront. This eliminates the overhead of pinning/unpinning user pages
> +and translating virtual addresses per i/o request. This also allows servers to
> +use the same registered buffers for subsequent backing store I/O (eg
> +read_fixed/write_fixed), keeping data in the same pinned pages without
> +additional pinning or mapping overhead required.
> +
> +Registered buffers require the buffer pool (FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL) to be
> +enabled. To use registered buffers, the server needs to register the
> +payload buffers through io-uring and during registration, set the
> +FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFFERS init flag (alongside the
> +FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag) in the registration sqe. For all sqes the
> +server passes in (including the registration ones), it needs to follow
> +standard protocol for using registered buffers (eg set
> +sqe->uring_cmd_flags to include IORING_URING_CMD_FIXED, and pass in the
> +index where the registered buffers reside to sqe->buf_index). For an example
> +of how to set this up, please refer to the libfuse/lib/fuse_uring.c file.
> +
> +Zero-copy
> +=========
> +
> +Fuse io-uring zero-copy allows the server to directly read from / write to
> +the client's pages, bypassing any intermediary buffer copies. This requires
> +the FUSE_URING_ZERO_COPY flag and requires the server to use registered
> +buffers. This requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges as zero-copy allows the server
> +direct access to the client's underlying pages, rather than operating on an
> +intermediary buffer that the contents of client's pages were copied into or on
> +page cache folios.
> +
> +When a fuse request arrives, the kernel registers the relevant pages (pinned
> +user pages for direct i/o or underlying page cache folios for buffered i/o)
> +into a sparse slot in the server's io_uring registered buffer table. The
> +server can then operate on these pages directly using io-uring fixed buffer
> +operations (eg read_fixed / write_fixed) and the kernel unregisters these
> +pages when the request completes. Non-page-backed args (eg op out headers)
> +will go through the payload buffer as normal.
> +
> +The request flow for the zero-copy write path (client writes data, server
> +reads it) is as follows:
> +
> +Zero-copy write
> +---------------::
> +|  Kernel                                   |  FUSE server
> +|                                           |
> +|  "write(fd, buf, 1MB)"                    |
> +|                                           |
> +|  >sys_write()                             |
> +|    >fuse_file_write_iter()                |
> +|      >fuse_send_one()                     |
> +|        [req->args->in_pages = true]       |
> +|        [folios hold client write data]    |
> +|                                           |
> +|  >fuse_uring_copy_to_ring()               |
> +|    >copy_header_to_ring(IN_OUT)           |
> +|      [memcpy fuse_in_header]              |
> +|    >copy_header_to_ring(OP)               |
> +|      [memcpy write_in header]             |
> +|                                           |
> +|    >fuse_uring_args_to_ring()             |
> +|      >setup_fuse_copy_state()             |
> +|        [skip_folio_copy = true]           |
> +|                                           |
> +|      >fuse_uring_set_up_zero_copy()       |
> +|        [folio_get for each client folio]  |
> +|        [build bio_vec array from folios]  |
> +|        >io_buffer_register_bvec()         |
> +|          [register pages at               |
> +|                ent->zero_copy_index]      |
> +|        [ent->zero_copied = true]          |
> +|                                           |
> +|      >fuse_copy_args()                    |
> +|        [skip_folio_copy => return 0       |
> +|         for page arg, skip data copy]     |
> +|                                           |
> +|    >copy_header_to_ring(RING_ENT)         |
> +|      [memcpy ent_in_out]                  |
> +|    >io_uring_cmd_done()                   |
> +|                                           |
> +|                                           | [CQE received]
> +|                                           |
> +|                                           | [issue io_uring READ at
> +|                                           |  ent->zero_copy_index]
> +|                                           | [reads directly from
> +|                                           | client's pages (ZERO_COPY)]
> +|                                           |
> +|                                           | [write data to backing
> +|                                           | store]
> +|                                           |  [submit COMMIT AND FETCH]
> +|                                           |
> +|  >fuse_uring_commit_fetch()               |
> +|    >fuse_uring_commit()                   |
> +|      >fuse_uring_copy_from_ring()         |
> +|    >fuse_uring_req_end()                  |
> +|      >io_buffer_unregister(ent->zero_copy_index) |
> +|        [unregister sparse buffer]         |
> +|      >fuse_zero_copy_release()            |
> +|        [folio_put for each folio]         |
> +|      [ent->zero_copied = false]           |
> +|      >fuse_request_end()                  |
> +|        [wake up client]                   |
> +
> +The zero-copy read path is analogous.
> +
> +Some requests may have both page-backed args and non-page-backed args.
> +For these requests, the page-backed args are zero-copied while the
> +non-page-backed args are copied to the buffer selected from the buffer
> +pool:
> +  zero-copy: pages registered via io_buffer_register_bvec()
> +  non-page-backed: copied to payload buffer via fuse_copy_args()
> +
> +For a request whose payload is zero-copied, the registration/unregistration
> +path looks like:
> +
> +register:  fuse_uring_set_up_zero_copy()
> +	     folio_get() for each folio
> +	     io_buffer_register_bvec(ent->zero_copy_index)
> +
> +unregister: fuse_uring_req_end()
> +	      io_buffer_unregister(ent->zero_copy_index)
> +	      -> fuse_zero_copy_release() callback
> +		 folio_put() for each folio

review_comments.author: Miklos Szeredi
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Miklos Szeredi expressed uncertainty about the buffer passing approach, noting that it simplifies logic at multiple layers but may limit dynamic capacity addition, and requested input from Joanne Koong on her preference.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 5 Jun 2026 at 00:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:

> In my opinion, passing in the buffers contiguously as a pool leads to
> simpler and cleaner logic at the fuse, io-uring, and userspace layers.
> If a daemon wants to dynamically add/remove buffer capacity in the
> future, they can add/remove another buffer pool or another ring,
> depending on their workload.
>
> From Bernd's perspective, passing in each buffer individually allows
> daemons who want to dynamically add/remove buffer capacity in the
> future to add/remove capacity one buffer at a time based on load
> instead of needing to commit upfront to adding/removing a pool.
>
> Could you please share your preference? This series is targeting the
> quickly approaching 7.2 merge window.

I simply lack context to make a good decision right now.  I can dive
into this next week...

Thanks,
Miklos

version_history.subject: [PATCH 6/6 v3] mm/memcontrol: remove unused memcg_stock code

series_items.subject: [PATCH 5/6 v3] mm/memcontrol: optimize memsw stock for cgroup v1
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the way memory usage is tracked in cgroup v1 by introducing a new, independent stock for the memsw page counter. This allows for better caching and reduces the number of hierarchical charges required when both memsw and memory page counters are over their limits. The change may cause reported memsw usage to temporarily be lower than memory usage due to batching of stock charges.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/6 v3] mm/memcontrol: convert memcg to use page_counter_stock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts memcg to use page_counter_stock, removing special casing for !allow_spinning and allowing page_counter_alloc_stock() to fail if memory is insufficient. It also restructures drain_all_stock to iterate CPUs in the outer loop, reducing synchronous per-CPU work calls from O(memcgs * CPUs) to just O(CPUs). The changes do not affect obj_stock or memsw stock, which will be handled separately.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/6 v3] mm/page_counter: introduce stock drain APIs
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces two new APIs, page_counter_drain_stock_local and page_counter_drain_stock_cpu, to replace the existing memcg stock draining functions. The local drain function takes a lock to serialize against concurrent charges, while the CPU drain function does not take a lock as it is only called from the CPU hotplug path where the CPU is dead and cannot have any more charges.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/6 v3] mm/page_counter: use page_counter_stock in page_counter_try_charge
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the page_counter_try_charge function to use the page_counter_stock feature when available. It first checks if there is enough stock to fulfill the charge request, and if so, charges the stock directly without attempting a hierarchical charge. If there is not enough stock, it attempts a greedy hierarchical charge and then tries to add any surplus pages to the stock. The patch preserves the existing semantics of the stock handling logic in try_charge_memcg.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/6 v3] mm/page_counter: introduce per-page_counter stock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a per-page_counter stock to the Linux kernel's memory management system, allowing each page counter to have its own cache of pre-charged pages. This addresses two issues: the random eviction of memcgs when handling more than 7 memcgs, and the tight coupling between stock and memcg, which prevents new page counters from using separate stocks. The patch adds a generic per-cpu stock struct and its allocation, disabling, and freeing functions, but does not yet use these features.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 0/6 v3] mm/memcontrol, page_counter: move stock from mem_cgroup to page_counter
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series moves the per-cpu stock from mem_cgroup to page_counter, consolidating stock limit checking and page_counter limit checking into a single function. This change eliminates the 7-memcg-per-cpu slot limit, random evictions (drain & refill), and slot traversal. The resulting code in memcg is also easier to follow, as caching becomes transparent from memcg's perspective and managed entirely within page_counter.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary: Raised the issue of synchronous draining work and its potential impact on system behavior, specifically the WARN_ON due to flushing a !wq_mem_reclaim wq. Proposed three options: trust that it's OK, use memcg_wq marked WQ_MEM_RECLAIM, or go back to asynchronous.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch removes unused code related to handling memcg_stock in the Linux kernel's mm/memcontrol module. The code was moved to page_counter_stock, so it is no longer needed. The removal of this code simplifies the kernel and reduces its complexity. The obj_stock logic remains untouched as it is still required. FLUSHING_CACHED_CHARGE is preserved for use by obj_stock.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 3/3] x86/tdx: Fix zero-extension for 32-bit port I/O

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 3/3] x86/tdx: Fix zero-extension for 32-bit port I/O

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a bug in the TDX (Trusted Domain Extensions) code for x86 architecture, specifically related to port I/O operations. The current implementation of handle_in() function only masks the lower 32 bits, which is incorrect according to x86 architecture rules. The patch uses insn_assign_reg() to write the result back into RAX with proper partial-register-write semantics.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 2/3] x86/insn-eval: Add insn_assign_reg() helper
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new helper function called insn_assign_reg() to the x86 instruction evaluation module. The function writes a value into a register with x86 partial-register-write semantics, which means it handles different byte sizes and zero-extension correctly. The function is added to a header file so that both KVM's instruction emulator and TDX guest #VE handler can use it. The patch also rewrites the helper function using arithmetic instead of pointer punning to make it more portable. There is no functional change in this patch, which means it only adds a new helper function without changing any existing behavior.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 1/3] x86/tdx: Fix off-by-one in port I/O handling

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 1/3] x86/tdx: Fix off-by-one in port I/O handling

patches_submitted.patch_summary: The patch fixes an off-by-one error in the port I/O handling code for TDX (Trusted Domain eXtensions) on x86 architecture. The issue arises from incorrect mask calculation using GENMASK, which results in a one-bit too wide mask for all I/O sizes. This is fixed by adjusting the mask calculation to exclude the high bit.
