patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 4/4] arm64: route crash_smp_send_stop() last resort through SDEI

version_history.subject: [PATCH 4/4] arm64: route crash_smp_send_stop() last resort through SDEI

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds the SDEI (System Description Event Interface) as a last resort to stop CPUs in an arm64 system when normal methods fail. It signals event 0 at still-online CPUs, which runs crash_save_cpu() on the wedged context and parks them. This approach is chosen because an SDEI-stopped CPU never completes its event, making it less recoverable than a normal stop. The patch adds functionality to handle this scenario and includes changes to various kernel files.

review_comments.author: Doug Anderson
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that the SDEI cross-CPU NMI handler captures crash_save_cpu() state from the wedged context before parking the CPU, but this may not be sufficient to ensure recoverability since an SDEI-stopped CPU never completes its event.

Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that the patch adds SDEI as a last resort for crash_smp_send_stop(), but not for regular smp_send_stop(), and suggested modifying smp_send_stop() to fall back to SDEI if sending the NMI failed, instead of adding this separate path.

Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that passing a cpumask_t to the stack in crash_smp_send_stop() can lead to issues due to its potential size under certain CONFIG options, and suggested avoiding this by defining it as static.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi,

On Wed, Jun 3, 2026 at 7:36 AM Kiryl Shutsemau <kirill@shutemov.name> wrote:

---

It feels weird to me that you're adding SDEI for "crash stop" but not
for regular "stop". It feels like you should modify smp_send_stop() to
fall back to SDEI if sending the NMI failed, instead of adding this
separate path.

---

The above will probably get you a yell. Putting "cpumask_t" on the
stack is a no-no since it can be quite large under certain CONFIG
options. This is why it's nearly always defined as "static".

-Doug

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author acknowledged that the escalation logic already lives in smp.c and agreed to restructure the patch in v2, addressing concerns about the handling of wedged CPUs during reboot.

Author acknowledged a potential code duplication issue in the IPI and SDEI handlers, agreed to refactor the code by extracting a shared helper function.

The author acknowledged a concern about the function being non-static and promised to make it static in v2, indicating that a fix is planned.
review_comments.raw_body:
Fair. A wedged CPU ignores the reboot-path stop just the same, and the
escalation logic already lives in smp.c, so I'll restructure in v2.

One thing to sort out there: this patch parks the stopped CPU inside
its SDEI handler without completing the event, which is fine for the
crash case (nothing expects the CPU back before reset), but a generic
stop path probably wants SDEI_EVENT_COMPLETE_AND_RESUME into a parking
stub instead, so that e.g. a regular kexec can bring all CPUs back up
in the new kernel. I'll look into that as part of the rework.

---

Yes -- falls out of the above. I will look into this.

Maybe pull the save/offline/park body into a shared helper that both the
IPI handler and the SDEI handler call.

---

Doh! Will make it static in v2 -- safe here since the path is serialized
by the crash_stop guard.

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 3/4] arm64: wire SDEI NMI into the hardlockup watchdog

version_history.subject: [PATCH 3/4] arm64: wire SDEI NMI into the hardlockup watchdog

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds support for the SDEI (System Description Event Interface) NMI to the hardlockup watchdog on arm64 architectures. It introduces a new driver that uses SDEI to deliver cross-CPU NMIs, which can be used by various kernel features such as arch_trigger_cpumask_backtrace and the hardlockup watchdog backend. The patch also selects HAVE_HARDLOCKUP_DETECTOR_ARCH in Kconfig to enable this feature. If the firmware provides SDEI, it is used; otherwise, a perf-NMI counter fallback is employed.

review_comments.author: Doug Anderson
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that the hardlockup watchdog backend is selected based on whether SDEI firmware is provided, but the fallback to a perf-NMI counter should be reconsidered because it may not work correctly in all cases.

Reviewer Doug Anderson questioned the necessity of the patch, suggesting that it duplicates functionality already present in the existing buddy hardlockup detector and patches #1 and #2 in the series.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi,

On Wed, Jun 3, 2026 at 7:36 AM Kiryl Shutsemau <kirill@shutemov.name> wrote:

---

I'm a little confused about this patch. We already have a buddy
hardlockup detector using the hrtimer, and it's even been improved
recently to trigger in a smaller time bound. It looks as if you're
duplicating bits of the perf and buddy detector here?

I don't think you need this patch at all. The existing buddy detector
+ patches #1 and #2 in your series should be sufficient.

Did I misunderstand?

-Doug

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the patch adds boot-time source selection, which is out of scope for this patchset, and agreed to drop it in v2.
review_comments.raw_body:
You're mostly right.

Buddy + #2 covers the console case (the remote branch triggers the
culprit's backtrace, which #2 makes deliverable), and #4 gets the wedged
CPU's registers into the vmcore.

The one thing this patch adds that a config can't is boot-time source
selection: PERF-compiled kernels have no detector on a pseudo_nmi=0
boot, and PREFER_BUDDY costs the pseudo-NMI machines perf
self-detection. But that's arguably out of scope for the patchset.

I'll drop this patch in v2 and run PREFER_BUDDY here. If a runtime
perf->buddy fallback ever materializes, the gap closes entirely.

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

review_comments.author: Doug Anderson
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the SDEI NMI handler is not properly synchronized, and requested the use of a spinlock to protect access to the hrtimer

Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that the hardlockup watchdog backend, when using SDEI NMI, has a limitation where it cannot detect simultaneous lockups of all CPUs and suggested an alternative solution to use a hardware watchdog timer's 'bark' interrupt as a NMI source.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi,

On Fri, Jun 5, 2026 at 2:12 PM Kiryl Shutsemau <kirill@shutemov.name> wrote:

---

Sure. If you're interested in trying to make pref vs. buddy coexist,
that should be done in a platform-agnostic way. Feel free to post
patches for that. I know we discussed this previously. Ah, here they
are:

https://lore.kernel.org/r/20250916145122.416128-1-wangjinchao600@gmail.com

I think those got bikeshedded to death and nobody cared enough to keep pushing.

FWIW, my belief is that the buddy detector is superior in every way
except that it can't detect when all CPUs lock up simultaneously.
...though I wonder if a nicer way to solve the "all CPUs locked up" is
to just NMI-enable the "bark" interrupt of a hardware watchdog timer.
That ought to be quite easy...

-Doug

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 2/4] drivers/firmware: add SDEI cross-CPU NMI service for arm64

version_history.subject: [PATCH 2/4] drivers/firmware: add SDEI cross-CPU NMI service for arm64

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a cross-CPU NMI service for arm64 using the SDEI software-signalled event mechanism, allowing an NMI-like event to be delivered to an interrupt-masked CPU without the pseudo-NMI hot-path cost. The first user of this feature is arch_trigger_cpumask_backtrace(), which can now reach masked CPUs without falling back to a plain IPI path. The patch includes new header files and code for registering a handler for SDEI event 0, as well as stubs for the cross-CPU NMI provider hooks. When SDEI is absent or disabled, the patch falls back to the original IPI path.

review_comments.author: Doug Anderson
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the SDEI cross-CPU NMI service may not be able to reach CPUs in EL2 mode, and requested a check for this case.

Reviewer suggested improving code readability by breaking up a long comment block into two separate blocks, each describing a different condition and its corresponding action.

Reviewer Doug Anderson questioned the need for an explicit suggestion that users say 'N' when SDEI is not available, considering it would be beneficial to have this feature even if SDEI is present.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi,

On Wed, Jun 3, 2026 at 7:36 AM Kiryl Shutsemau <kirill@shutemov.name> wrote:

---

nit: instead of one comment block, I would have broken it up in two. Like:

/*
 * Prefer the SDEI ...
 */
if (sdei_nmi_trigger_cpumask_backtrace(mask, exclude_cpu))
  return;

/*
 * NOTE: though ...
 */
nmi_trigger_cpumask_backtrace(...);

---

Is there some downside to this? It seems like anyone who has the SDE
interface would want this. Not sure why you'd suggest people say "N".

Other than the nit, this looks reasoanble to me, though I'm a complete
noob when it comes to SDEI...

Reviewed-by: Douglas Anderson <dianders@chromium.org>

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a concern about the 'say N' warning, explaining it's cautionary and not a technical limit, but is open to changing the default recommendation or adding a default y in v2 if that's the consensus.
review_comments.raw_body:
No real downside -- without the software-signalled event the driver
stays inert, and there is no cost until an event actually fires.

The "say N" is caution, not a technical limit: so far this has run on
QEMU (TF-A) and one hardware platform, and the interesting paths depend
on each vendor's SDEI implementation at EL3. I'm not sure vendors would
care to run SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL validation. Maybe we want to see more
data points first?

But maybe I am too cautious. Happy to flip the recommendation (or add
default y) in v2 if that the consensus.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 1/4] firmware: arm_sdei: add SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL support
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for the SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL call, which signals a software-signalled event to another PE in an NMI-like manner. The new function sdei_event_signal() is added as a thin wrapper over the SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL call and is safe to call from NMI or crash context.

review_comments.author: Doug Anderson
review_comments.summary: The patch looks reasonable to him, although he hasn't thoroughly reviewed it due to lack of prior knowledge about SDEI. He gave the patch a positive review with a Reviewed-by tag.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi,

On Wed, Jun 3, 2026 at 7:36 AM Kiryl Shutsemau <kirill@shutemov.name> wrote:

>
> Add sdei_event_signal(), a thin wrapper over the SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL call
> (DEN0054) that makes the software-signalled event (event 0) pending on a

> It takes no locks, so it is safe to call from NMI / crash context.

I'd never looked at SDEI before this (so my review is probably not
terribly strong), but this looks reasonable to me.

Reviewed-by: Douglas Anderson <dianders@chromium.org>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/4] arm64: cross-CPU NMI via SDEI
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new mechanism for delivering NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupts) across CPUs on arm64 architecture, using the SDEI (System Description Event Infrastructure) protocol. The goal is to enable features like hard-lockup detection and crash reporting without incurring significant performance overhead. Currently, two existing mechanisms are available: pseudo-NMI, which has a hot-path cost, and FEAT_NMI, which is not present in deployed silicon. This new approach uses SDEI events to deliver interrupts, allowing for efficient interrupt masking and reducing the impact on system performance.

review_comments.author: Doug Anderson
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that the SDEI event signal handler does not properly handle the case where a CPU is already in an NMI context, and requested that the code be modified to check for this condition before calling sdei_event_signal().

Reviewer Doug Anderson questioned the necessity of the SDEI-based cross-CPU NMI mechanism, suggesting that it duplicates functionality already provided by the buddy hardlockup detector and its recent improvements.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi,

On Wed, Jun 3, 2026 at 7:36 AM Kiryl Shutsemau <kirill@shutemov.name> wrote:

---

I'm a little confused about this patch. We already have a buddy
hardlockup detector using the hrtimer, and it's even been improved
recently to trigger in a smaller time bound. It looks as if you're
duplicating bits of the perf and buddy detector here?

I don't think you need this patch at all. The existing buddy detector
+ patches #1 and #2 in your series should be sufficient.

Did I misunderstand?

-Doug

review_comments.author: Doug Anderson
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that the SDEI event signal call is not properly synchronized, which can lead to a race condition between the sdei_event_signal() wrapper and the firmware's dispatch of the handler NMI-like.

Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that the patch only adds SDEI for 'crash stop' but not for regular 'stop', and suggested modifying smp_send_stop() to fall back to SDEI if sending the NMI failed, instead of adding a separate path.

Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that using cpumask_t on the stack could lead to issues due to its potential size under certain CONFIG options, and suggested defining it as static instead.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi,

On Wed, Jun 3, 2026 at 7:36 AM Kiryl Shutsemau <kirill@shutemov.name> wrote:

---

It feels weird to me that you're adding SDEI for "crash stop" but not
for regular "stop". It feels like you should modify smp_send_stop() to
fall back to SDEI if sending the NMI failed, instead of adding this
separate path.

---

The above will probably get you a yell. Putting "cpumask_t" on the
stack is a no-no since it can be quite large under certain CONFIG
options. This is why it's nearly always defined as "static".

-Doug

review_comments.author: Doug Anderson
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that the patch does not handle the case where SDEI is disabled, which would prevent the NMI from being delivered to the target CPU.

Reviewer Doug Anderson expressed a positive sentiment towards the patch, stating that it 'looks reasonable' and providing a Reviewed-by tag.
sentiment_signals: patch lacks a fallback for when SDEI is disabled
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi,

On Wed, Jun 3, 2026 at 7:36 AM Kiryl Shutsemau <kirill@shutemov.name> wrote:

---

I'd never looked at SDEI before this (so my review is probably not
terribly strong), but this looks reasonable to me.

Reviewed-by: Douglas Anderson <dianders@chromium.org>

review_comments.author: Doug Anderson
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that the patch does not handle the case where SDEI is disabled, which would prevent the cross-CPU NMI mechanism from working.

Reviewer Doug Anderson suggested breaking up a comment block into two separate blocks to improve readability, specifically separating the SDEI preference from the subsequent code

Reviewer Doug Anderson questioned the necessity of this patch, suggesting that anyone with the SDE interface would want it and expressing confusion as to why people would opt out.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi,

On Wed, Jun 3, 2026 at 7:36 AM Kiryl Shutsemau <kirill@shutemov.name> wrote:

---

nit: instead of one comment block, I would have broken it up in two. Like:

/*
 * Prefer the SDEI ...
 */
if (sdei_nmi_trigger_cpumask_backtrace(mask, exclude_cpu))
  return;

/*
 * NOTE: though ...
 */
nmi_trigger_cpumask_backtrace(...);

---

Is there some downside to this? It seems like anyone who has the SDE
interface would want this. Not sure why you'd suggest people say "N".

Other than the nit, this looks reasoanble to me, though I'm a complete
noob when it comes to SDEI...

Reviewed-by: Douglas Anderson <dianders@chromium.org>

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: The author acknowledges that the patch is not fully addressing the console case and getting registers into vmcore, but notes that these are covered by other patches (#2 and #4). They agree to drop this patch in v2 and run PREFER_BUDDY instead, which closes the gap with a runtime perf->buddy fallback.
review_comments.raw_body:
You're mostly right.

Buddy + #2 covers the console case (the remote branch triggers the
culprit's backtrace, which #2 makes deliverable), and #4 gets the wedged
CPU's registers into the vmcore.

The one thing this patch adds that a config can't is boot-time source
selection: PERF-compiled kernels have no detector on a pseudo_nmi=0
boot, and PREFER_BUDDY costs the pseudo-NMI machines perf
self-detection. But that's arguably out of scope for the patchset.

I'll drop this patch in v2 and run PREFER_BUDDY here. If a runtime
perf->buddy fallback ever materializes, the gap closes entirely.

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the potential downsides of using SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL, specifically the need for vendor-specific validation and testing. They acknowledge the caution but express uncertainty about vendors' willingness to run such tests, suggesting that more data points are needed before making a decision.
review_comments.raw_body:
No real downside -- without the software-signalled event the driver
stays inert, and there is no cost until an event actually fires.

The "say N" is caution, not a technical limit: so far this has run on
QEMU (TF-A) and one hardware platform, and the interesting paths depend
on each vendor's SDEI implementation at EL3. I'm not sure vendors would
care to run SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL validation. Maybe we want to see more
data points first?

But maybe I am too cautious. Happy to flip the recommendation (or add
default y) in v2 if that the consensus.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author acknowledged that a wedged CPU ignores the reboot-path stop and agreed to restructure in v2, addressing concerns about the escalation logic and SDEI event handling.

Author acknowledged a potential issue with code duplication between IPI and SDEI handlers, agreed to refactor by extracting a shared helper function.

The author acknowledged a concern about the dynamic allocation of a static variable and agreed to make it static in version 2, ensuring safety due to serialization by the crash_stop guard.
review_comments.raw_body:
Fair. A wedged CPU ignores the reboot-path stop just the same, and the
escalation logic already lives in smp.c, so I'll restructure in v2.

One thing to sort out there: this patch parks the stopped CPU inside
its SDEI handler without completing the event, which is fine for the
crash case (nothing expects the CPU back before reset), but a generic
stop path probably wants SDEI_EVENT_COMPLETE_AND_RESUME into a parking
stub instead, so that e.g. a regular kexec can bring all CPUs back up
in the new kernel. I'll look into that as part of the rework.

---

Yes -- falls out of the above. I will look into this.

Maybe pull the save/offline/park body into a shared helper that both the
IPI handler and the SDEI handler call.

---

Doh! Will make it static in v2 -- safe here since the path is serialized
by the crash_stop guard.

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

review_comments.author: Doug Anderson
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that the patch does not handle the case where the SDEI event is delivered to a CPU that has already panicked, which would cause the kdump to be corrupted.

Reviewer Doug Anderson suggested that the buddy detector, which can't detect simultaneous CPU lockups, could be improved by NMI-enabling a hardware watchdog timer's 'bark' interrupt.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi,

On Fri, Jun 5, 2026 at 2:12 PM Kiryl Shutsemau <kirill@shutemov.name> wrote:

---

Sure. If you're interested in trying to make pref vs. buddy coexist,
that should be done in a platform-agnostic way. Feel free to post
patches for that. I know we discussed this previously. Ah, here they
are:

https://lore.kernel.org/r/20250916145122.416128-1-wangjinchao600@gmail.com

I think those got bikeshedded to death and nobody cared enough to keep pushing.

FWIW, my belief is that the buddy detector is superior in every way
except that it can't detect when all CPUs lock up simultaneously.
...though I wonder if a nicer way to solve the "all CPUs locked up" is
to just NMI-enable the "bark" interrupt of a hardware watchdog timer.
That ought to be quite easy...

-Doug
